s5.title: Compliance with Personal Data Protection Principles
s5.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will verify that the organisation has a functioning compliance programme covering all seven principles, with demonstrable accountability at senior management level. The absence of a DPO, a compliance policy, or a ROPA are among the most reliable indicators of systemic non-compliance that inspectors look for.

evidenceItems.name: Record of Processing Activities (ROPA)
evidenceItems.description: A comprehensive register of all personal data processing activities conducted by the organisation, mapping each activity to its applicable principles, lawful basis, data categories, purposes, and responsible owner
whatGoodLooksLike: ROPA is maintained in a structured format (spreadsheet or purpose-built tool) with a named owner for each entry
Every processing activity has an identified lawful basis under s6(2) with supporting documentation
ROPA shows the last-reviewed date and is updated whenever new processing activities commence
Processing activities are mapped to all seven principles with notes on how compliance is achieved
ROPA version history is maintained showing evolution over time
commonGaps: ROPA has not been updated since initial creation and no longer reflects actual processing activities
ROPA exists but contains no lawful basis analysis — just a list of data types
Processing activities added after the initial ROPA creation are not recorded
ROPA is a single document with no version control or last-reviewed date
No named owner for individual processing activities, making accountability unclear
suggestedSources: ROPA document or spreadsheet (current version and prior versions)
Change log or version history for the ROPA
Data governance meeting minutes approving ROPA updates
evidenceItems.retentionPeriod: Current version plus previous 3 years of versions

evidenceItems.name: PDPA Compliance Policy (Board-Approved)
evidenceItems.description: A formal policy document approved by the Board or equivalent governing body articulating the organisation's commitment to PDPA compliance, the seven principles, and the accountability framework including the DPO appointment
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy is approved and signed by the Board Chair or CEO with a clear approval date
Policy explicitly addresses each of the seven Personal Data Protection Principles
Named accountability for each principle and for overall PDPA compliance is specified
Policy includes a review schedule (at least annual) and shows evidence of previous reviews
Policy is communicated to all staff and accessible on the organisation's intranet or equivalent
commonGaps: No Board-approved policy exists; only operational-level procedures
Policy exists but has not been reviewed or updated since the 2024 Amendment came into force
Policy is generic and does not reference the specific PDPA principles or Malaysian legal requirements
Policy is not communicated to staff and cannot be shown to have been distributed
Policy exists but accountability assignments are vacant or names persons who have since left the organisation
suggestedSources: Board resolution or minutes approving the PDPA compliance policy
Signed PDPA compliance policy document (current version)
Distribution records or email showing policy communication to staff
Intranet screenshot showing policy is published and accessible
evidenceItems.format: PDF / Word document with Board approval evidence
evidenceItems.retentionPeriod: Current version plus all prior versions indefinitely

evidenceItems.name: Annual Compliance Audit Report
evidenceItems.description: A report documenting the results of an annual audit of PDPA compliance across all seven principles, including findings, gap analysis, and remediation actions
whatGoodLooksLike: Audit covers all seven principles with specific findings and evidence for each
Report identifies gaps with risk ratings and assigns remediation actions to named owners with target dates
Findings from prior year's audit include status updates showing remediation progress
Audit methodology is documented, including systems reviewed, staff interviewed, and documents examined
Report is presented to senior management or Board with formal acknowledgement
commonGaps: No annual compliance audit has ever been conducted
Audit covers only a subset of principles (e.g., only security and consent) without addressing all seven
Findings from prior audits remain open with no evidence of remediation
Audit is self-assessed by the same team responsible for compliance without independent review
Report exists but was never presented to management, suggesting it was not acted upon
suggestedSources: Annual compliance audit report (most recent and prior year)
Audit scope document and methodology
Management sign-off on audit report
Remediation tracker showing status of prior findings

auditTips: Ask for the ROPA and test a sample of 5 processing activities by requesting supporting documentation for the claimed lawful basis
Check the DPO appointment letter — is the DPO named in the ROPA? Can they explain the seven principles?
Ask when the PDPA compliance policy was last reviewed and whether this was after the 2024 Amendment (effective April 2025)
Request the last Board or senior management meeting minutes where PDPA compliance was reported

s6.title: General Principle
s6.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will verify that the organisation has a documented lawful basis for each personal data processing activity, with particular attention to consent records and their validity. The 2024 Amendment's strengthened consent definition means any consent collected before April 2025 should be reviewed against the new standard.

evidenceItems.name: Consent records database or log
evidenceItems.description: Centralised system or records showing when, how, and what consent was obtained from each data subject, including the specific purposes consented to
whatGoodLooksLike: Timestamped consent records with a clear link to the specific processing purposes consented to
Records show the exact wording or version of the consent form presented to each data subject
Separate consent records for separate processing purposes (granular consent)
Consent records include the method of collection (web form, paper, verbal with follow-up)
Withdrawal records are integrated, showing when consent was withdrawn and what processing stopped
commonGaps: Consent collected via pre-ticked boxes — records exist but consent is invalid under the 2024 Amendment definition
No record of when or how consent was obtained; only a flag showing 'consented: yes'
Single bundled consent record for multiple unrelated purposes with no granularity
Consent records show data subject provided consent but no record of what wording they were shown
Consent records are maintained per system rather than centrally, creating gaps across the organisation
suggestedSources: CRM system consent module export
Website or app consent management platform (CMP) audit log
Paper consent forms (scanned and indexed) with collection date
Marketing database opt-in/opt-out history export

evidenceItems.name: Lawful basis register
evidenceItems.description: A document mapping each processing activity in the ROPA to its s6(2) lawful basis, with supporting evidence for each basis claimed
whatGoodLooksLike: Each processing activity is mapped to a specific subsection of s6(2) (e.g., s6(2)(a) — contract, s6(2)(b) — legal obligation)
For contract basis: a sample contract evidencing the data subject's party status is referenced
For legal obligation basis: the specific law or regulation requiring the processing is identified
For legitimate interests basis: a documented Legitimate Interest Assessment (LIA) is attached
Register is reviewed at least annually and upon any new processing activity
commonGaps: Processing activities list 'consent' as the lawful basis but no consent records can be produced
Multiple processing activities claim 'legitimate interests' basis without any LIA
Register exists but is incomplete — only covers some processing activities
Lawful basis claimed does not match the actual circumstances (e.g., claiming 'contract' for marketing activities not required under any contract)
Register has never been reviewed since initial creation
suggestedSources: Lawful basis register (standalone or as column in ROPA)
Legitimate Interest Assessments for LI-based processing
Sample consent forms evidencing the standard used
Legal opinions or analyses supporting claimed legal obligation bases

evidenceItems.name: Consent withdrawal mechanism and records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that data subjects can withdraw consent and that withdrawals are processed promptly with cessation of the relevant processing
whatGoodLooksLike: Unsubscribe/opt-out links in marketing communications are functioning and lead to an immediate withdrawal mechanism
Withdrawal request log shows requests received and the date processing ceased for each
Time between withdrawal request and cessation of processing does not exceed a reasonable period (e.g., 48 hours for electronic communications)
Withdrawal of consent triggers notification to third parties who received the data under the same consent
Self-service withdrawal mechanism is as easy to use as the original opt-in
commonGaps: Unsubscribe links in emails are broken or lead to a 404 error
Withdrawal requests are acknowledged but processing does not actually stop (e.g., suppression list not applied to all marketing lists)
No mechanism for withdrawing consent for non-marketing processing purposes
Third parties are not notified of withdrawal of consent, so they continue processing based on the same original consent
No log of withdrawal requests and their outcomes
suggestedSources: Marketing platform suppression list and unsubscribe history
Withdrawal request log from CRM or DPO inbox
Test of unsubscribe mechanism (live test during inspection)
Emails to third parties notifying of consent withdrawals
evidenceItems.format: System screenshots / Database export / Email evidence

auditTips: Request a sample of 20 consent records across different processing purposes and verify each against the four elements of valid consent: freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous
Test the unsubscribe link in a recent marketing email during the inspection to verify it functions
Ask for the Legitimate Interest Assessment for the most significant LI-based processing activity and assess whether the three-part test was properly applied
Check whether consent forms were updated after the 2024 Amendment came into force (April 2025) to reflect the new definition of consent

s7.title: Notice and Choice Principle
s7.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will review all privacy notices in use across the organisation's data collection channels against the s7(1) checklist. Common findings include missing bilingual notices, outdated notices that do not reflect current processing, and notices that omit mandatory elements such as the right of access or contact details.

evidenceItems.name: Privacy notice / Personal Data Protection Notice (current versions — BM and English)
evidenceItems.description: The current versions of the organisation's privacy notice in both Bahasa Malaysia and English, covering all s7(1) elements, for each data collection channel
whatGoodLooksLike: Both BM and English versions are substantively identical and dated with the last-updated date
Notice explicitly covers all s7(1) elements: description of data collected, purposes, third party recipients, access/correction rights, contact details, obligatory/voluntary status
Notice is accessible at every data collection point (website footer, registration form, paper form header)
Plain language is used — no excessive legal jargon; a layperson can understand the key information
Version history is maintained showing previous versions and when changes were made
commonGaps: Notice is available in English only, without a Bahasa Malaysia version
Notice omits one or more mandatory s7(1) elements (most commonly: consequences of non-provision, or contact details for access/correction requests)
Notice has not been updated to reflect new processing purposes or third-party disclosures introduced after the notice was last published
Notice is published on the website but is not actively brought to the attention of data subjects at the point of collection
BM and English versions differ in substance (e.g., English version contains additional disclosures not in BM version)
suggestedSources: Website privacy policy (current and archived versions via Wayback Machine or internal version control)
Mobile app privacy notice screenshots
Paper consent and data collection forms
Point-of-sale or in-store notice boards (photos)

evidenceItems.name: Notice distribution and communication records
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that privacy notices were actively communicated to data subjects at the point of collection and that existing data subjects were informed of material changes to the notice
whatGoodLooksLike: Email notifications to existing customers/data subjects when a material privacy notice update was made, with a link to the updated notice
For web-based collection: registration forms include a checkbox or notice requiring the data subject to acknowledge the privacy notice before submission
For paper collection: signed forms where the data subject's signature acknowledges receipt of the notice
Notification campaigns for significant changes (e.g., new processing purposes) are documented with send dates and recipient counts
commonGaps: Privacy notice is updated on the website but existing data subjects are never notified of changes
Online registration forms do not include any reference to the privacy notice — the notice is buried in the website footer only
No record exists of when any version of the notice was communicated to any data subject
Paper forms do not include the privacy notice — they only include a statement that 'a privacy policy exists on our website'
suggestedSources: Email campaign records showing privacy notice update communications to data subjects
Registration form screenshots showing privacy notice acknowledgement
Signed paper consent forms with notice acknowledgement
CRM records showing notice version acknowledged by each data subject
evidenceItems.format: Email records / CRM screenshots / Signed forms
evidenceItems.retentionPeriod: Duration of data subject relationship + 7 years

evidenceItems.name: Opt-out / choice mechanism evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that data subjects have been provided with a genuine and functioning choice to opt out of non-essential processing, particularly direct marketing
whatGoodLooksLike: Marketing consent mechanism clearly separates essential (service delivery) consent from optional (marketing) consent, with distinct checkboxes
Opt-out mechanism is as prominent and accessible as the corresponding opt-in
Marketing platform shows suppressed/opted-out contacts and demonstrates that they receive no marketing communications
Opt-out processing time from request to suppression is documented and consistently applied
JPDP test: a request to opt out during the inspection is processed within the committed timeframe
commonGaps: A single checkbox covers consent to both service delivery and marketing, with no separate opt-out for marketing
Opt-out is technically available but requires multiple steps or a written request, making it effectively inaccessible
Opted-out contacts continue to receive some categories of marketing communications (e.g., opted out of email but still receive SMS)
Opt-out preferences are not synchronised across all marketing channels and platforms
suggestedSources: Screenshots of consent mechanism in registration forms or checkout flows
Marketing platform export showing opted-out contacts and their opt-out dates
Opt-out processing SLA document
Multi-channel suppression consistency test results
evidenceItems.retentionPeriod: Duration of data subject relationship + 7 years

auditTips: Use the s7(1) element checklist against the actual privacy notices — verify each of the 7 required elements is present in both BM and English
Check the date on the privacy notice — was it updated after the 2024 Amendment came into force? If not, does the existing notice satisfy the new requirements?
Navigate to the organisation's website and test the registration journey — is the privacy notice visible and acknowledged before data submission?
Ask to see the opt-out mechanism and test whether it functions immediately

s8.title: Disclosure Principle
s8.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will focus on third-party data sharing, particularly whether data processing agreements are in place and whether disclosures align with what was notified to data subjects. Cross-border transfers are a particular area of focus following the 2024 Amendment's introduction of s129.

evidenceItems.name: Third-party disclosure register
evidenceItems.description: A register documenting all third parties to whom personal data has been disclosed, the categories of data shared, the purpose, the legal basis, and the contractual mechanism
whatGoodLooksLike: Register identifies each third party by name, the categories of personal data shared, the purpose, and the specific s8(1) basis or exception
DPA reference number is recorded for each third-party recipient who is a data processor
Register is updated whenever a new third-party disclosure arrangement is established
Register is reconciled against the privacy notice — all categories of recipients listed in the register should be reflected in the notice
Cross-border transfers are flagged with the applicable s129 mechanism
commonGaps: No third-party disclosure register exists — the organisation cannot identify who receives its personal data
Register exists but is significantly incomplete — major vendors receiving personal data (e.g., cloud providers, analytics platforms) are not listed
Register does not distinguish between data processors (who require a DPA) and independent data controllers (who require data subject consent)
Third-party recipients listed in the privacy notice do not match the register
suggestedSources: Third-party disclosure register (standalone or as part of ROPA)
Vendor management system listing all vendors with data access
Procurement records showing vendors engaged to process personal data
IT systems inventory showing all integrations and API connections to third-party systems

evidenceItems.name: Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) with data processors
evidenceItems.description: Executed written agreements with all data processors, covering the mandatory terms required by s43 and consistent with the disclosure obligations under s8
whatGoodLooksLike: DPAs are executed with all data processors identified in the processor register, and all DPAs predate the commencement of processing
DPAs contain mandatory clauses: instruction-only processing, confidentiality, security standards, sub-processor controls, breach notification, and post-contract deletion
DPAs are current (not expired), and renewal dates are tracked
DPAs reference the specific categories of personal data to be processed and the permitted processing purposes
DPAs include provisions specific to cross-border transfers where the processor is located overseas
commonGaps: No DPAs in place with major data processors (e.g., cloud hosting provider, CRM vendor, payroll processor)
DPAs exist but are general-purpose NDAs without the specific data protection clauses required by s43
DPAs were executed after processing had already commenced, meaning there was a period of processing without contractual protection
DPAs have expired and have not been renewed, leaving an ongoing relationship without contractual protection
DPAs do not contain breach notification obligations, preventing the processor from notifying the data controller of incidents
suggestedSources: Executed DPA documents for all processors (latest signed version)
Processor register cross-referenced with DPA file to show 100% coverage
DPA renewal calendar and tracking records
Legal review sign-off on DPA templates used

auditTips: Request the full list of vendors/third parties with access to personal data and cross-reference against DPA files — verify there are no gaps
Review a sample of 5 DPAs against the s43 requirements checklist — check particularly for breach notification, sub-processor controls, and deletion obligations
Compare third-party recipients listed in the privacy notice against the third-party disclosure register to verify alignment

s9.title: Security Principle
s9.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors assess the Security Principle against the Personal Data Protection Standards 2015 (Security Standard), which sets specific requirements for both electronic and non-electronic processing. The 2024 Amendment's breach notification requirements under s12B make security governance even more critical — inspectors will also look for incident response readiness.

evidenceItems.name: Data Security Policy
evidenceItems.description: A written, senior management-approved security policy covering the protection of personal data from loss, misuse, modification, unauthorised access, disclosure, alteration, and destruction
whatGoodLooksLike: Policy is approved and signed by senior management (CEO or CISO) with a clear approval and review date
Policy addresses both electronic and non-electronic personal data processing
Policy includes specific requirements: encryption, access controls, incident response, staff training, and third-party processor security
Policy has been reviewed and updated after the 2024 Amendment came into force, incorporating breach notification obligations
Policy is communicated to all relevant staff and referenced in staff contracts or employment terms
commonGaps: No written data security policy exists — only an IT security policy that does not specifically address personal data
Policy exists but was approved more than 2 years ago and has not been reviewed since
Policy does not address non-electronic (paper) personal data — treats security as an IT-only concern
Policy does not include breach notification procedures, leaving staff without guidance on incident reporting
Policy is not communicated to staff — only held on file by the CISO and unknown to most of the organisation
suggestedSources: Data security policy document (current version with approval signature)
Policy version history and review log
Staff communication records showing policy distribution
Policy acknowledgement records from staff training
evidenceItems.format: PDF / Word document with approval signature
evidenceItems.retentionPeriod: Current version plus all prior versions for 10 years

evidenceItems.name: Security risk assessment reports and remediation tracker
evidenceItems.description: Documented security risk assessments conducted periodically for systems processing personal data, with an active remediation tracker for identified vulnerabilities
whatGoodLooksLike: Risk assessment covers all systems processing personal data, with specific findings related to personal data risks
Assessment methodology is documented and calibrated to the sensitivity of data processed
Remediation tracker shows status of all findings from the last assessment, with target resolution dates and responsible owners
High and critical findings are remediated within 30 days; medium findings within 90 days
Penetration test reports are included for internet-facing systems processing personal data
Previous year's findings are shown as closed with evidence of remediation
commonGaps: No security risk assessment has been conducted in the past 2 years
Risk assessment exists but does not specifically address personal data risks — focuses only on general IT risks
Critical vulnerabilities identified in risk assessments remain open with no remediation timeline
Penetration testing has never been conducted on systems containing personal data
Remediation tracker exists but shows overdue items with no escalation or management attention
suggestedSources: Most recent security risk assessment report
Penetration test reports (most recent, for each in-scope system)
Vulnerability remediation tracker (showing open and closed findings)
Board or management sign-off on risk acceptance for any unresolved high/critical findings

evidenceItems.name: Security controls evidence (encryption, access controls, MFA)
evidenceItems.description: Technical evidence demonstrating that required security controls are implemented and operating effectively, including encryption at rest and in transit, role-based access controls, and multi-factor authentication
whatGoodLooksLike: Encryption certificates for all databases containing personal data showing AES-256 or equivalent at rest
TLS/SSL configuration screenshots for all web-facing and API services processing personal data (minimum TLS 1.2)
Access control matrix showing role-based access with least-privilege and named users for each system
MFA enforcement evidence for all privileged and remote access accounts
Quarterly access review records showing user accounts reviewed and removed/modified where appropriate
Device encryption evidence for laptops and mobile devices (e.g., BitLocker or equivalent policy)
commonGaps: Encryption at rest is not applied to the primary customer database or HR system
Some systems still use TLS 1.0 or 1.1 which are deprecated and vulnerable
MFA is not enforced for remote access — only password authentication is required
Access reviews are conducted annually rather than quarterly, and the last review showed significant numbers of former staff still with active accounts
Mobile devices used to access personal data are not encrypted or enrolled in MDM
suggestedSources: Database encryption configuration screenshots or certificates
SSL Labs or equivalent TLS configuration scan results for web services
IAM system access control matrix export
MFA enrollment report from identity provider
Quarterly access review completion records
MDM enrollment and device encryption compliance reports

evidenceItems.name: Security awareness training records
evidenceItems.description: Records showing that all staff with access to personal data have completed mandatory security awareness training, including modules relevant to personal data protection
whatGoodLooksLike: Training completion records show 100% participation among staff with access to personal data in the past 12 months
Training curriculum covers: phishing awareness, social engineering, clean desk policy, data handling, and incident reporting
Training records include completion dates, pass scores (if assessed), and the training version completed
New staff receive security training as part of onboarding before being granted access to systems containing personal data
Training materials are reviewed and updated to reflect current threats and PDPA amendments
commonGaps: No formal security awareness training programme exists — reliance on informal briefings only
Training completion records are incomplete or cannot be produced for all staff
Training has not been updated since 2023 and does not include modules on breach notification obligations under s12B
Training is conducted for IT staff only and does not reach customer-facing or administrative staff who handle personal data
Training is a one-time onboarding event with no annual refresh
suggestedSources: Training management system completion report
Training materials (course content, slides)
Staff completion certificates or acknowledgement records
New staff onboarding checklist showing security training as a prerequisite for system access
evidenceItems.format: Training management system report / PDF certificates

auditTips: Request access to the Personal Data Protection Standards 2015 (Security Standard) self-assessment checklist and ask the CISO to walk through it
Ask for the most recent penetration test report and focus on any high or critical findings — are they remediated?
Request the access review records from the last quarter — how many accounts were modified or removed?
Ask: 'What happens if a staff member loses a laptop containing customer data?' — the answer reveals the breach response process and device encryption policy

s10.title: Retention Principle
s10.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will look for a documented retention schedule and evidence that it is being actively applied. Particular scrutiny is placed on whether personal data is being retained indefinitely without a documented legal basis, and whether deletion/disposal is being carried out in a secure manner.

evidenceItems.name: Data Retention Schedule
evidenceItems.description: A documented schedule specifying the retention period for each category of personal data, the legal or business basis for the period, and the approved disposal method
whatGoodLooksLike: Schedule covers all categories of personal data processed by the organisation, cross-referenced with the ROPA

Each retention period is justified with a specific legal, regulatory, or business basis (e.g., 'Employment records: 7 years post-termination — Employment Act 1955 s60D' or 'Customer transaction records: 7 years — Income Tax Act requirement')
Disposal method is specified for each category (e.g., 'certified electronic deletion', 'cross-cut shredding', 'incineration')
Schedule distinguishes between active retention (data being used) and archival retention (data no longer actively used but retained for legal reasons)
Schedule has been reviewed by Legal counsel and approved by the DPO
commonGaps: No retention schedule exists — the organisation retains all personal data indefinitely by default
Retention schedule exists but is aspirational and has never been operationalised (no automated controls or regular disposal runs)
Schedule specifies retention periods but no disposal method, leaving disposal ad hoc and undocumented
Schedule does not cover all data categories — omits data held in email archives, backups, or legacy systems
Retention periods are not justified with legal bases — described as 'business needs' without specific reasoning
suggestedSources: Data Retention Schedule document (current version with legal counsel sign-off)
Legal review or memorandum supporting retention periods
ROPA cross-reference showing all data categories are covered by the schedule
evidenceItems.retentionPeriod: Current version plus prior versions for 7 years

evidenceItems.name: Disposal certificates and deletion logs
evidenceItems.description: Records documenting the secure disposal or deletion of personal data that has reached the end of its retention period, including the data category, disposal date, method, and authorising officer
whatGoodLooksLike: Disposal certificates issued by a certified data destruction vendor for physical records (with serial number, destruction date, and method)
Electronic deletion logs from data management systems showing data purged with timestamps and the retention policy applied
Deletion runs are conducted on a regular schedule (e.g., monthly or quarterly) and documented
Backups and archives are subject to the same retention schedule as live data, with disposal logs covering backup deletion
Disposal process is signed off by the data owner before execution
commonGaps: No disposal has ever occurred — systems contain personal data from the organisation's inception with no documented deletion
Electronic deletion occurs but is not logged — no evidence of when data was deleted or what was deleted
Certified destruction certificates cannot be produced for physical records destroyed by a third-party vendor
Backups and email archives are explicitly excluded from the retention schedule — data is retained indefinitely in these locations
Deletion is conducted by IT on an ad hoc basis without authorisation from the data owner or DPO
suggestedSources: Certified destruction certificates from data destruction vendors (last 3 years)
System deletion logs or audit trails showing automated or scheduled purges
Data owner authorisation records for disposal runs
Backup management policy showing backup retention and deletion schedule

auditTips: Ask to see the oldest customer record in the primary CRM — is the retention period for that customer's data justified?
Check whether backups are covered by the retention schedule — a very common gap is personal data being retained indefinitely in backup archives
Request a disposal certificate from the last physical record destruction and verify the destruction method is appropriate for the sensitivity of the data

s11.title: Data Integrity Principle
s11.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will assess whether the organisation has reasonable processes to maintain data accuracy, including validation at point of collection, update mechanisms, and a functioning correction request process. Data quality reports and correction request logs are the primary evidence items.

evidenceItems.name: Data quality controls documentation and test results
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that data quality controls are implemented in systems processing personal data, including validation rules, format checks, and duplicate detection
whatGoodLooksLike: System configuration documentation showing input validation rules applied at collection (e.g., phone number format validation, email format check, mandatory field enforcement)
Duplicate detection configuration in CRM showing how duplicate contacts are identified and merged
Data quality audit reports showing the percentage of records with missing or invalid fields
Regular data quality reports presented to the DPO showing key metrics (e.g., percentage of records with valid contact details)
Process for systematic update of records when data subjects inform the organisation of changes
commonGaps: No input validation at collection — data entry allows any format for key fields, resulting in large numbers of unusable or inaccurate records
Data quality has never been formally measured and no data quality reports have been produced
Duplicate records abound in the CRM with no deduplication process, resulting in multiple conflicting records for the same data subject
No systematic process for updating data when data subjects notify the organisation of changes to their contact details or other information
suggestedSources: System configuration screenshots showing validation rules
Data quality audit report (most recent)
CRM duplicate detection configuration
Process document for handling data update notifications from customers

evidenceItems.name: Data correction request log and outcomes
evidenceItems.description: Records of all data correction requests received from data subjects, the nature of the claimed inaccuracy, the organisation's response, and the outcome
whatGoodLooksLike: Correction request log records all requests received with date, nature of inaccuracy, response date, outcome, and third parties notified
Log shows response times within the 21-day period (s32 standard)
Where corrections were made, evidence shows the correction was propagated to all relevant systems and third parties notified
Where corrections were disputed, the log shows a correction statement was attached to the record with the data subject's version
Log is reviewed by the DPO monthly for systemic patterns indicating process gaps
commonGaps: No correction request log exists — requests are handled informally with no record
Log exists but shows a significant backlog of unanswered requests beyond the 21-day period
Corrections are made in the primary system but not propagated to downstream systems or third parties
Where corrections were disputed and a correction statement should have been attached, no such annotation is applied
Log is maintained by the customer service team without any DPO review or oversight
suggestedSources: Data correction request log (last 12 months)
Evidence of correction propagation (screenshots showing correction in multiple systems)
Correspondence to third parties notifying of corrections
Correction statement attachments where corrections were disputed

auditTips: Run a sample data quality check on a system containing personal data — ask to see the percentage of records with missing mandatory fields
Select 5 data subjects who have previously submitted correction requests and trace the correction through all systems to verify propagation
Ask to see a correction statement annotation on a record where the correction was disputed — if it cannot be produced, this is a compliance gap

s12.title: Access Principle
s12.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will assess whether the organisation has published and operationalised an accessible data subject access and correction request process. They will test the organisation's ability to respond within the 21-day statutory period and produce evidence of past request handling.

evidenceItems.name: Data Subject Access and Correction Request Procedure (published)
evidenceItems.description: The documented and published procedure for data subjects to submit access and correction requests, including the contact details, required format, processing timeframe, and any applicable fee
whatGoodLooksLike: Procedure is published in both BM and English, accessible from the privacy notice or organisational website
Procedure clearly states the 21-day response timeframe and any applicable fee
Contact details for submitting requests are current and actively monitored
Procedure specifies the information required from the requestor for identity verification
Internal procedure document details the steps the organisation takes from receipt to response
commonGaps: No published procedure — privacy notice states access requests can be submitted but provides no contact details or process
Published contact email for access requests is unmonitered or goes to a generic inbox with no defined owner
Procedure does not state the 21-day timeframe, leaving data subjects uncertain of when to expect a response
Identity verification requirements are so onerous (e.g., requiring notarised copies of documents) that they effectively deter legitimate requests
Internal procedure is undocumented — requests are handled differently by different staff depending on who receives them
suggestedSources: Published data subject access request procedure (website screenshot)
Privacy notice section on data subject rights
Internal access request handling procedure document
Published request form or template

evidenceItems.name: Data subject access and correction request log
evidenceItems.description: A log recording all data subject access and correction requests received, with dates, outcomes, and response times, demonstrating compliance with the 21-day statutory period
whatGoodLooksLike: Log captures all requests across all channels (email, post, in-person, phone follow-up)
Log shows date received, date acknowledged, date of full response, outcome, and any extension requests
Response times for all requests in the log are within 21 days, with documented justification for any that required extension
Where access was refused, the log records the specific exemption relied on and evidence that written notification was provided to the data subject
Log is reviewed by the DPO quarterly and reported to management
commonGaps: No request log exists — requests are handled informally on an ad hoc basis with no records
Log exists but shows multiple requests that were not responded to within 21 days without any extension request
Log is incomplete — only email requests are captured; requests submitted via post or in-person are not recorded
Log shows requests were responded to within the timeframe but the response content is not logged (no copy of the response retained)
Requests that resulted in refusal do not show evidence that written notification of refusal was provided
suggestedSources: Data subject request log (last 3 years)
Sample access request responses (redacted)
Written refusal notifications where applicable
Extension requests submitted to the Commissioner where applicable

auditTips: Navigate to the organisation's website and attempt to find the data subject access request procedure — is it easy to find? Are the contact details current?
Request the access request log for the past 2 years and verify all response times are within 21 days
Select 3 sample access requests and request copies of the actual responses provided to verify completeness
Test the contact email address listed in the privacy notice — does someone respond within a reasonable timeframe?

s13.title: Registration of Data Controllers
s13.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will verify the organisation's registration status at the outset of any inspection. A lapsed, expired, or absent registration when required is an immediate finding of serious non-compliance. Inspectors will also verify that the registered particulars match the organisation's actual processing activities.

evidenceItems.name: Current PDPA Registration Certificate
evidenceItems.description: The organisation's current, valid registration certificate issued by the Commissioner, showing the registration number, covered industry sector, and expiry date
whatGoodLooksLike: Certificate is current (not expired) and issued to the correct legal entity
Certificate covers all relevant industry sectors in which the organisation operates
Certificate is accessible and can be produced immediately during an inspection
Renewal application is on file showing the renewal was submitted before the expiry of the previous certificate
Digital copy is stored in the compliance document repository and a physical copy is held by the Company Secretary
commonGaps: Certificate has expired and no renewal has been submitted — the organisation is processing personal data without valid registration
Certificate covers only some of the industry sectors in which the organisation operates, leaving some processing activities unregistered
Certificate is held by a former employee and the current organisation cannot locate it
Certificate is for a predecessor legal entity and has not been updated to reflect a corporate restructuring or name change
suggestedSources: Original PDPA Registration Certificate (physical and digital copy)
Renewal application and Commissioner's acknowledgement
JPDP online portal screenshot confirming current registration status

evidenceItems.name: Registration renewal compliance calendar and reminder evidence
evidenceItems.description: Evidence of a compliance calendar system that tracks the registration expiry date and generates advance renewal reminders
whatGoodLooksLike: Compliance calendar shows the registration expiry date with automated reminders set at 90, 60, and 30 days
Evidence that renewal reminders were sent and acted upon for the current registration period
Named responsible officer for initiating the renewal process with documented backup
Renewal is initiated at least 60 days before expiry to allow for processing time
Historical evidence shows the organisation has consistently renewed before expiry
commonGaps: No compliance calendar — the organisation relies on the responsible officer's memory to track renewal dates
Compliance calendar exists but the reminder emails were filtered as spam and not acted upon
Renewal was submitted late (after expiry) because the responsible officer was on leave and no backup was designated
No record of previous renewal cycles — the organisation cannot demonstrate a history of timely renewals
suggestedSources: Compliance calendar screenshots showing registration expiry date and reminders
Email reminders sent to responsible officer
Historical renewal applications and Commissioner acknowledgements
Named responsible officer and backup documented in compliance manual
evidenceItems.format: Calendar screenshots / Email evidence / Process document

auditTips: Request the PDPA Registration Certificate at the start of the inspection — this is the first item on the JPDP inspection checklist
Verify the certificate is in the name of the correct legal entity (registered company name matches the certificate)
Check the expiry date — if it has expired, ascertain when the renewal application was submitted and whether a gap period exists
Cross-reference the industry sector on the certificate against the organisation's actual business activities

s14.title: Application for Registration
s14.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will verify that the registration application accurately reflects the organisation's processing activities. Where the application is inconsistent with actual operations, inspectors will assess whether this represents an error, a failure to notify changes, or an attempt to misrepresent processing activities.

evidenceItems.name: Registration application and ROPA consistency review
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that the information provided in the most recent registration application is consistent with the organisation's actual processing activities as documented in the ROPA
whatGoodLooksLike: The processing activities, data categories, and purposes described in the registration application match those in the ROPA
Where the ROPA was updated after the registration application was submitted, a change notification to the Commissioner is on file
Legal counsel or DPO sign-off on the registration application pre-submission is documented
No material processing activities are described in the ROPA but absent from the registration application
commonGaps: Registration application describes significantly fewer processing activities than the ROPA — many activities were added since registration without change notification
Registration application was prepared without reference to the ROPA, resulting in inconsistencies
Change notifications have never been filed despite material changes to processing activities since the last registration
No DPO or legal review of the registration application was conducted before submission
suggestedSources: Copy of the most recent registration application submitted to JPDP
ROPA (current version) for cross-reference
Change notification letters sent to the Commissioner
DPO or legal counsel sign-off on the application

auditTips: Compare the registration application against the ROPA — are there processing activities in the ROPA that are not mentioned in the application?
Ask when the organisation last reviewed the registration application for accuracy and what triggered the review
Check whether any corporate events (restructuring, name change, new business lines) occurred after registration that should have triggered a change notification

s30.title: Right of Access to Personal Data
s30.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors may submit a test data access request during or after an inspection to verify the organisation's response capability. They will also review past request logs to verify compliance with the 21-day statutory deadline. The format, completeness, and language of responses are assessed.

evidenceItems.name: Sample access request responses
evidenceItems.description: Copies of responses provided to data subjects in response to data access requests, demonstrating that the organisation provides complete and intelligible information within the 21-day period
whatGoodLooksLike: Response includes all personal data held about the data subject across all relevant systems
Information is provided in an intelligible format with explanatory labels (not just a raw database dump)
Response is in a language the data subject can understand (BM or English as appropriate)
Response is dated within 21 days of the original request
Where some data was withheld, the response explains the specific exemption relied on and the data subject's right to complain
commonGaps: Response only covers data from one system while the data subject's information is spread across multiple systems (incomplete response)
Response is a raw database export that is incomprehensible to a layperson without technical knowledge
Response is provided in English only to a data subject who communicated in Bahasa Malaysia
Response time exceeds 21 days with no Commissioner extension request on file
Refusals do not provide reasons or information about the right to complain, which is itself a procedural breach
suggestedSources: Redacted sample access request responses (last 12 months)
Data subject request log showing response dates
Response template used by the organisation
Evidence of identity verification conducted before response (to show personal data was only disclosed to the correct person)

auditTips: Submit a test data access request during or after the inspection and assess whether the response is received within 21 days and is complete
Select 3 sample responses from the request log and assess completeness — does the response cover all systems listed in the data map?
Ask the organisation to demonstrate, live during the inspection, how a data access request would be handled from receipt to response

s31.title: Compliance with Data Access Request
s31.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will review the request log to verify that all requests were responded to within 21 days, and that any extensions were properly applied for. They will also check whether any fees charged are within the permitted amount.

evidenceItems.name: Data subject request management system or log with deadline tracking
evidenceItems.description: A systematic record of all data subject requests showing receipt dates, response dates, and evidence of deadline monitoring
whatGoodLooksLike: Every request has a clearly recorded date received and date responded, showing elapsed time
Automated deadline alerts are configured at day 14 and day 20 with evidence of alerts having been sent
Where 21-day deadline was not met, an extension request to the Commissioner is on file predating the deadline
No requests have been simply abandoned or ignored
Request log is reviewed by the DPO monthly and presented in the quarterly compliance report to management
commonGaps: Request log shows multiple instances where the 21-day deadline was missed with no Commissioner extension request
Request log is maintained in a shared email inbox rather than a structured tracking system, making it impossible to verify all requests are captured
Deadline tracking is manual — there are no automated alerts, and deadlines are frequently missed as a result
Log shows 'completed' for requests that were only partially fulfilled without explanation of what was withheld
suggestedSources: Data subject request management system export (all requests, last 3 years)
Automated deadline alert configuration and alert history
Extension requests submitted to the Commissioner
Monthly DPO report on request handling performance

auditTips: Count the days between 'date received' and 'date responded' for every entry in the request log — identify any exceeding 21 days
For any 21-day deadline breach, verify whether an extension request was submitted to the Commissioner before the deadline
Ask whether the organisation charges a fee for access requests, and if so, verify the fee is within the prescribed amount

s32.title: Right to Correct Personal Data
s32.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will review the correction request log and test whether corrections are propagated to all relevant systems. The requirement to notify third parties of corrections is frequently overlooked — inspectors will specifically look for evidence of third-party notification.

evidenceItems.name: Data correction request log with outcomes and third-party notifications
evidenceItems.description: A complete record of all data correction requests received, showing the outcome (corrected, correction statement attached, or refused with reasons) and evidence of notification to third parties where relevant
whatGoodLooksLike: Log captures all correction requests with date received, nature of claimed inaccuracy, response, and outcome
Where corrections were made, evidence exists of the change in the primary system and notification to any relevant third parties
Where corrections were disputed, evidence exists of the correction statement annotation on the record
Third-party notification is documented with email or letter evidence showing the notification was sent and received
DPO reviews correction request outcomes monthly to identify systemic data quality issues
commonGaps: Corrections are made in the primary CRM but the corrected information is not pushed to integrated systems (e.g., the marketing platform, billing system, or customer portal retain the old data)
Third parties who received the inaccurate data are never notified of the correction
Where the organisation disputes the correction, no correction statement is attached to the record
No correction request log exists — corrections are handled via ad hoc emails to the customer service team
suggestedSources: Correction request log (last 3 years)
Before/after screenshots showing data correction in primary and downstream systems
Email or letter correspondence notifying third parties of corrections
Correction statement annotations on disputed records

auditTips: Select 3 correction request log entries and trace each correction through all downstream systems — is the corrected data consistent across all systems?
Ask the organisation to identify the third parties who received the inaccurate data in each sample case and show evidence of notification
Look for correction statement annotations on disputed records — this is a specific requirement that is often not implemented

s43.title: Processing by Data Processor
s43.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will verify that all data processors engaged by the organisation are covered by written Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) and that those DPAs contain the mandatory terms. A gap in DPA coverage for even a single significant processor is treated as a serious compliance finding.

evidenceItems.name: Processor register and DPA coverage matrix
evidenceItems.description: A register of all data processors engaged by the organisation, cross-referenced with executed DPAs, showing 100% DPA coverage
whatGoodLooksLike: Processor register lists all processors with: processor name, description of processing service, categories of personal data processed, DPA reference and date, and SLA for breach notification
Every processor in the register has a corresponding executed DPA dated before the commencement of processing
Processor register is reviewed quarterly and updated when new processors are engaged or relationships end
DPA coverage matrix clearly shows that no processor has access to personal data without a valid DPA
The register cross-references the ROPA and the third-party disclosure register to confirm consistency
commonGaps: Register exists but major processors are missing (e.g., the cloud hosting provider, email service provider, or HR system vendor are not listed as processors)
DPAs are in place for some processors but the organisation cannot identify when each DPA was signed relative to when processing commenced
Processor register has not been updated in over a year and no longer reflects the current vendor landscape
Some processors are classified as 'independent data controllers' (not requiring a DPA) when they are actually processing on the organisation's behalf (and do require a DPA)
suggestedSources: Processor register (current version)
IT vendor list cross-referenced with the processor register
Executed DPAs for all processors (file of signed agreements)
Procurement records showing all vendors given access to personal data

evidenceItems.name: Sample DPAs reviewed against s43 requirements checklist
evidenceItems.description: Evidence that executed DPAs contain all mandatory terms required by s43, including instruction-only processing, confidentiality, security standards, sub-processor controls, breach notification, and post-contract deletion
whatGoodLooksLike: DPAs explicitly restrict the processor to processing personal data only on the organisation's documented instructions
DPAs include confidentiality obligations binding all of the processor's personnel who handle personal data
DPAs specify minimum security standards at least equivalent to the organisation's own security requirements
DPAs require the processor to notify the organisation of any data breach within a defined timeframe (typically 24-48 hours)
DPAs require deletion or return of all personal data at the end of the processing engagement
DPAs restrict sub-processing without prior written consent from the data controller
commonGaps: DPAs are general-purpose service agreements or NDAs without specific data protection clauses — they do not address any of the s43 requirements
DPAs do not include breach notification obligations — the organisation has no contractual mechanism to be notified of processor-side breaches
DPAs permit sub-processing without restriction, meaning the processor can engage sub-processors the data controller is unaware of
DPAs do not include post-contract deletion obligations — personal data may remain on processor systems after the relationship ends
DPA templates used are out of date and do not reflect the 2024 Amendment requirements
suggestedSources: Sample DPAs (at least 5, from different processor types)
DPA compliance checklist used by Legal to review DPAs
Standard DPA template approved by Legal counsel for use in new processor engagements

evidenceItems.name: Processor due diligence review records
evidenceItems.description: Records of periodic due diligence reviews of data processors to verify that they are implementing the security measures required by their DPA
whatGoodLooksLike: Annual due diligence questionnaires sent to each significant processor, with responses on file
Security certifications (e.g., ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II) from processors are on file and current
Where processor due diligence reveals non-compliance with the DPA, a remediation plan is documented and monitored
Due diligence findings are reported to the DPO and CISO
For critical processors, on-site audit rights under the DPA have been exercised at least once
commonGaps: No processor due diligence has ever been conducted — DPAs are signed but compliance with them is never verified
Due diligence is limited to collecting a security questionnaire but the responses are never reviewed or followed up
ISO 27001 or SOC 2 certificates on file are expired (more than 12 months old)
A processor disclosed a breach to the data controller but the data controller did not escalate or investigate the implications for its own compliance
suggestedSources: Annual processor due diligence questionnaire responses
Processor security certifications (ISO 27001 certificates, SOC 2 reports)
Due diligence review reports
Correspondence with processors regarding compliance concerns
Processor audit reports where on-site audits were conducted

auditTips: Ask for the processor register and cross-reference it against the IT vendor list — are all vendors with access to personal data listed?
Select 5 DPAs from different types of processors (cloud, marketing, HR, logistics) and check each against the s43 requirements checklist
Ask: 'Has any processor notified you of a data breach?' — the answer reveals both the state of processor breach notification and the organisation's own breach awareness

s129.title: Cross-Border Transfer of Personal Data (2024 Amendment)
s129.auditorFocus: Following the 2024 Amendment, JPDP inspectors will specifically assess cross-border data transfer compliance under s129. The most common finding is inadvertent cross-border transfer through cloud services and SaaS platforms without a transfer mechanism. Data flow maps and transfer registers are the primary documents requested.

evidenceItems.name: Data flow map showing cross-border transfers
evidenceItems.description: A visual or tabular map of all personal data flows, specifically identifying any flows that cross Malaysia's borders, including cloud service provider data centres and SaaS platform data storage locations
whatGoodLooksLike: Data flow map identifies every system that stores or processes personal data and the country where data is stored
All cloud service providers are identified with their data centre locations
Map distinguishes between data stored/processed in Malaysia, data in approved countries (Transfer Order), and data in non-approved countries requiring a transfer mechanism
Each cross-border flow is annotated with the s129 mechanism relied on
Map is updated whenever new systems are deployed or cloud service providers change their data centre configurations
commonGaps: No data flow map exists — the organisation has no visibility of where its personal data is stored or processed
Data flow map covers only on-premises systems and does not capture cloud service or SaaS platform data locations
Map was prepared at a point in time and has not been updated as new SaaS platforms were adopted
Organisation is not aware that commonly used SaaS platforms (e.g., CRM, email marketing, video conferencing) store data in non-Malaysian data centres
suggestedSources: Data flow map (current version with date)
Cloud service provider Terms of Service or Data Processing Addenda showing data centre locations
IT asset and SaaS inventory showing all cloud services in use
Network architecture diagrams showing data flows

evidenceItems.name: Cross-border transfer register with applicable mechanisms
evidenceItems.description: A register documenting all transfers of personal data outside Malaysia, the destination country, the transfer mechanism relied on, and supporting documentation
whatGoodLooksLike: Register lists all cross-border transfers with: destination country, recipient/processor, data categories transferred, volume, transfer mechanism under s129, and date of first transfer
For each transfer relying on SCCs: executed SCC documents are referenced and on file
For each transfer relying on adequacy: the Transfer Order listing is referenced
For each transfer relying on consent: the specific consent records are referenced
Register is reviewed annually and updated when new transfers commence or mechanisms change
commonGaps: No cross-border transfer register — the organisation has not identified its cross-border transfers as a distinct compliance category
Register lists transfers but does not specify the s129 mechanism relied on for each
SCCs are referenced in the register but the executed SCC documents cannot be produced
Transfers to non-approved countries are listed in the register but the 'mechanism' column is blank or states 'N/A'
Register does not cover transfers that occur through cloud-based SaaS platforms used by staff (e.g., file-sharing with overseas colleagues via cloud storage)
suggestedSources: Cross-border transfer register (current version)
Executed Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) with overseas processors
Executed Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) for intra-group transfers
Personal Data Protection (Transfer of Personal Data to Places Outside Malaysia) Order (applicable list)
Consent records specifically covering cross-border transfer consent

auditTips: Ask whether the organisation uses Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, or other major cloud platforms — then ask where the data is stored and what the transfer mechanism is
Request the executed SCCs for the top 5 overseas data processors — when were they executed relative to when processing commenced?
Ask the DPO to identify the destination country of the organisation's cloud hosting provider — if they cannot answer immediately, it indicates the cross-border transfer mapping has not been completed

s12B.title: Data Breach Notification
s12B.auditorFocus: Inspectors will verify the organization's capability to detect breaches and notify the Commissioner 'as soon as practicable'. They will check for a functioning breach register and evidence of 72-hour notification readiness.

evidenceItems.name: Personal Data Breach Register
evidenceItems.description: A log of all security incidents involving personal data, including the assessment of risk and notification decisions.
whatGoodLooksLike: Includes timestamp of discovery and timestamp of notification
Records rationale for notifying or not notifying data subjects (harm test)

evidenceItems.name: Commissioner Breach Notification Records
evidenceItems.description: Copies of all notifications sent to the JPDP within the required timeframe.

s101.title: Powers of Inspection
s101.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will assess whether the organisation is prepared to cooperate with inspections and has a documented inspection response procedure. The state of the compliance document repository is a direct reflection of the organisation's overall compliance maturity.

evidenceItems.name: Compliance document repository (completeness and currency)
evidenceItems.description: A central repository containing all key PDPA compliance documents, organised and readily accessible to produce to JPDP inspectors promptly
whatGoodLooksLike: Repository contains all key documents: ROPA, registration certificate, DPO appointment, PDPA policy, DPAs, retention schedule, disposal logs, privacy notices, training records, breach log, access request log, and security policy
All documents are current (last-reviewed within the past 12 months, or within the applicable review cycle)
Documents are organised by category and retrievable within minutes
Repository is maintained in both physical (for key signed originals) and digital formats
Repository completeness is reviewed against a checklist quarterly by the DPO
commonGaps: Key compliance documents are scattered across multiple systems, email inboxes, and network drives with no central access
Repository exists but key documents are out of date (e.g., retention schedule from 2020, DPAs that have expired)
Repository completeness has never been formally reviewed against a checklist
Physical documents (signed originals of DPAs, registration certificate) cannot be located
suggestedSources: Compliance document repository (digital and/or physical)
Repository completeness checklist and last-review date
DPO quarterly repository review records
evidenceItems.retentionPeriod: Per the retention schedule applicable to each document type

evidenceItems.name: Inspection response procedure and inspection preparedness self-assessment
evidenceItems.description: A documented procedure for responding to JPDP inspections, and evidence of annual self-assessments conducted to identify and remediate compliance gaps before an actual inspection
whatGoodLooksLike: Inspection response procedure designates the DPO as primary contact and specifies notification of Legal counsel within hours of an inspection notice
Inspection response procedure assigns responsibilities for document collection, witness preparation, and escort of inspection officers
Annual self-assessment is conducted using the JPDP inspection checklist as a guide
Self-assessment findings are documented with remediation actions and completion dates
Self-assessment report is presented to management to resource gap remediation
commonGaps: No inspection response procedure exists — the organisation has not planned for how it would respond to an unannounced inspection
No self-assessments have been conducted — the organisation will discover its compliance gaps only during an actual inspection
Self-assessment was conducted but findings were not acted upon — the same gaps are still present at the time of the actual inspection
suggestedSources: Inspection response procedure document
Annual PDPA self-assessment report (last 2 years)
Remediation tracker from self-assessment
Management acknowledgement of self-assessment findings

auditTips: At the start of the inspection, ask the organisation to produce its compliance document index — assess how quickly documents are retrieved
Request the most recent self-assessment report and compare the findings with observations made during the current inspection — are the same gaps still present?
Ask who the DPO is and verify they are the same person named in the inspection response procedure and available to assist during the inspection

s104.title: Complaint Procedures
s104.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors may have specific data subject complaints they are investigating when conducting an inspection. They will look for evidence that the organisation has a functioning internal grievance mechanism, takes complaints seriously, and investigates and remediates genuine violations.

evidenceItems.name: Internal data subject complaint procedure (published)
evidenceItems.description: A documented and published internal complaint procedure enabling data subjects to raise PDPA concerns directly with the organisation before escalating to the Commissioner
whatGoodLooksLike: Complaint procedure is published in BM and English, accessible from the privacy notice or organisation's website
Procedure specifies a named contact or team for receiving complaints and a committed response timeframe
Procedure explains what data subjects should include in their complaint

Procedure explains what the organisation will do with the complaint (investigate, respond, remediate) and what the data subject can do if they are not satisfied with the outcome (escalate to Commissioner)
Procedure is tested periodically to verify the contact details are working
commonGaps: No internal complaint procedure published — data subjects are directed immediately to the Commissioner without any internal resolution opportunity
Complaint contact email or address is incorrect, unmonitered, or inactive
Procedure does not specify any timeframe for response, leaving data subjects in uncertainty
Procedure exists but is not linked from the privacy notice, making it inaccessible to most data subjects
suggestedSources: Published complaint procedure (website screenshot or PDF)
Privacy notice showing link to complaint procedure
Test email to complaint contact address confirming it is active

evidenceItems.name: Data subject complaint log and resolution records
evidenceItems.description: A log of all data subject complaints received (direct and via the Commissioner), showing investigation steps, outcomes, and remedial actions taken
whatGoodLooksLike: Complaint log records all complaints with date received, nature, investigation steps, outcome, and remedial actions
Complaints are investigated promptly with a target resolution timeframe
Where a complaint reveals a genuine violation, remediation is documented and the data subject is informed of the outcome
Complaint themes are analysed to identify systemic issues requiring structural remediation
DPO reviews complaint log monthly and reports complaint metrics to management
commonGaps: No complaint log exists — complaints are handled informally with no records
Log records complaints received but does not document investigation steps or outcomes
Where the complaint reveals a genuine violation, no remediation is documented
Complaint log shows a pattern of similar complaints (e.g., multiple complaints about unsubscribe mechanisms not working) that indicate a systemic issue that has not been addressed
suggestedSources: Complaint log (last 3 years)
Sample complaint investigation records and response letters
Remediation actions taken in response to complaints
Management report on complaint metrics

auditTips: Navigate to the organisation's website and attempt to find the complaint procedure — is it easily accessible? Are the contact details working?
Review the complaint log for patterns — recurring complaint types indicate systemic compliance issues that should have been addressed
Ask whether the organisation has received any complaints that were also lodged with the Commissioner — if so, verify the organisation cooperated fully with the Commissioner's investigation

s110.title: Enforcement Notices
s110.auditorFocus: If the Commissioner has previously issued an enforcement notice to the organisation, JPDP inspectors will specifically verify that the notice has been fully complied with. Evidence of compliance with every requirement specified in the notice must be produced. Partial or late compliance is treated as a continuing offence.

evidenceItems.name: Enforcement notice compliance evidence package
evidenceItems.description: A structured file containing the enforcement notice, the organisation's compliance plan, and evidence of completion of every remedial step specified in the notice, with dates
whatGoodLooksLike: Compliance plan was prepared and submitted to the Commissioner within the timeline specified in the notice
Each requirement in the notice has corresponding evidence of compliance (screenshots, system configuration records, policy documents, training records as applicable)
Compliance was confirmed to the Commissioner in writing before or by the deadline specified in the notice
All evidence is timestamped, demonstrating that remedial steps were taken before the deadline
Legal counsel review of the compliance evidence package is documented before submission to the Commissioner
commonGaps: Organisation received an enforcement notice but cannot produce evidence of any remedial steps taken
Remedial steps were taken after the deadline without seeking a Commissioner extension
Only some requirements from the notice were addressed — others were overlooked or deprioritised
Evidence of compliance exists but was never formally submitted to the Commissioner, leaving the compliance confirmation on file in the organisation only
suggestedSources: Copy of the enforcement notice received
Compliance plan prepared in response to the notice
Evidence package for each remedial requirement (tagged to the specific notice requirement)
Written confirmation of compliance submitted to the Commissioner
Commissioner acknowledgement of compliance

auditTips: Check the JPDP investigation history for the organisation — have any enforcement notices been issued? If so, verify full compliance evidence
Ask the DPO: 'Have you received any enforcement notices from the Commissioner?' — cross-reference the answer against the JPDP register if available
If an enforcement notice was received but compliance is claimed, test a sample of the remedial steps by independently verifying the technical changes were implemented

s12A.title: Appointment of Data Protection Officer
s12A.auditorFocus: JPDP inspectors will verify whether the organisation has formally appointed a DPO and notified the Commissioner as required by Section 12A(3). They will also assess whether the DPO has sufficient authority and resources to perform their functions independently.

evidenceItems.name: DPO Appointment Letter & Job Description
evidenceItems.description: Formal documentation of the DPO's appointment, clearly defining their roles, responsibilities, and reporting line to senior management.
whatGoodLooksLike: Document is signed by the CEO or Board
Explicitly mentions independence and avoidance of conflict of interest
Specifies duties aligned with JPDP DPO Guidelines 2025

evidenceItems.name: JPDP DPO Registration Confirmation
evidenceItems.description: Proof that the DPO appointment was notified to the Commissioner via the MyPDPA portal or prescribed form.

s43A.title: Right to Data Portability
s43A.auditorFocus: Inspectors will check whether the organization has a technical mechanism to transmit personal data to another controller in a structured format upon request.

evidenceItems.name: Data Portability Procedure & Technical Spec
evidenceItems.description: Documented process for handling portability requests and the data formats supported (e.g., CSV, JSON).

s34.title: Right to Correct Personal Data
s34.auditorFocus: Verification of the workflow for receiving and acting upon data correction requests within 21 days.

evidenceItems.name: Correction Request Log
evidenceItems.description: Log of all correction requests received and evidence of propagation to third parties.
